thermodynamics-bded6d08-a0fa-4051-84a3-3a2ece187286


First law of Thermodynamics

The idea of entropy was developed to quantify irreversability of a thermocynamic process.
The thermodynamic process was developed to describe the first Law of Thermodynamics. Its intent was to explain the operation of heat engines, the high technology of the Indestrial Revolution, by placing constraints on the work they could perform.
The arguments presented here were developed by Clausius and carnol the mid-ninteenth century. During this period concepts such as temperature and heat were phenonenolical concepts with no theoretical interpretations.
A thermodynamic process is a change in state of a thermodynamic system. It a thermodynamic system as a collection of particles confined to a volume.
The state of a thermodynamic system is described by
quantities such as temeserature, volume, pressure, particle number, internal energy and entropy. while volume, pressure and particle number have clear meaninas, temperature is assumed to be what is measured by a thermometer. Internal energy is related to the temperature of a thermodynamic system and is a measure of the energy contained in the motion of the porlicles that comprise a thermodynamic system. An interpreation of entropy will be developed here.
A relation between the variations in the quantities describing the state of the thermodynamic system is called an equation of State. For example if the pressure, volume and temperature D , V D , V D,VD, VD,V and T T TTT are the
variables an equalion of
state nas the form
s ( P , V , T ) = 0 s ( P , V , T ) = 0 s(P,V,T)=0s(P, V, T)=0s(P,V,T)=0
It follows that only two state
variables are independent. If P P PPP and v v vvv are assumed independent then

u = u ( P , v ) u = u ( P , v ) u=u(P,v)u=u(P, v)u=u(P,v)

which would describe the
variation in internal energy as the other state variables
change.
A thermodpinamic process has an intial stale and a final state where the transition between the states is assumed to conserve energy. This is the famaes First low of Thermodynamics,
Δ u = Q − w Δ u = Q − w Delta u=Q-w\Delta u=Q-wΔu=Q−w
where Δ u Δ u Delta u\Delta uΔu is the change in internal energy of the process, Q Q QQQ is the heat added or removed b. the process and ω ω omega\omegaω is the work perfomed by the process. Heat added is assumed positive and heat removed is negative Similarly work done by the process on the system is negative and positive if work is done by the system. The First law of Thermodynamics is a quantitive statement of intuitions about thermal systems, namely, that the energy changes that occur
in a thermodynamic system are equal to enersy recieued from the enviomoment in the forms of heat and work. As an example consider the case where Q = O Q = O Q=OQ=OQ=O. The consevation of energy becomes
Δ u = − ω Δ u = − ω Delta u=-omega\Delta u=-\omegaΔu=−ω
This describes a process that does mechanick work with no neat transper.
This process can be madeled by the following no dissipative system

where the system consists of a cylinder filled with a gas in state u 1 ( P i , v i ) u 1 P i , v i u_(1)(P_(i),v_(i))u_{1}\left(P_{i}, v_{i}\right)u1(Pi,vi) which is compressed by a piston piston, where P i P i P_(i)P_{i}Pi and V i V i V_(i)V_{i}Vi are the initial pressure and udume repectively. The walls of the cylinder are assumed to not conduet helat so that heat cannot enter or leave the gas in the cylinder.
This property is culled adibutic and can be thought of as a perfect thermos bottle. It is also assumed that the piston moves without dissipating energy. In the intral state a force F i F i F_(i)F_{i}Fi maintins the position of the system.
If the force is increased to a value of F ¯ f F ¯ f bar(F)_(f)\bar{F}_{f}F¯f the volveme of the cylinder will be decreased by an amount Δ v Δ v Delta v\Delta vΔv until a new position where pressure of the gas against the piston produces a force equal but in the opposite direction of F f F f F_(f)F_{f}Ff. The state of the gas will change to u ( P f , v f ) u P f , v f u(P_(f),v_(f))u\left(P_{f}, v_{f}\right)u(Pf,vf) where v f < v i v f < v i v_(f) < v_(i)v_{f}<v_{i}vf<vi and P f > P i P f > P i P_(f) > P_(i)P_{f}>P_{i}Pf>Pi.
The work done on the system is
− ω = − F f Δ z = − E f A A Δ z = − P Δ V − ω = − F f Δ z = − E f A A Δ z = − P Δ V -omega=-F_(f)Delta z=-(E_(f))/(A)A Delta z=-P Delta V-\omega=-F_{f} \Delta z=-\frac{E_{f}}{A} A \Delta z=-P \Delta V−ω=−FfΔz=−EfAAΔz=−PΔV
Where A A AAA is the crossectional area of the cylinder and P P PPP is the pressure excerted by the piston on the gas. It follows from the conservation of energy that the change in internal energy is given by
Δ U = − ω = − P Δ V Δ U = − ω = − P Δ V Delta U=-omega=-P Delta V\Delta U=-\omega=-P \Delta VΔU=−ω=−PΔV
In the infintesimal limit this becomes
d u = − P d V d u = − P d V du=-PdVd u=-P d Vdu=−PdV
The time required to perform this process is assumed to be long enough for the final state to be described by u ( P i , v i ) u P i , v i u(P_(i),v_(i))u\left(P_{i}, v_{i}\right)u(Pi,vi) any transients are assumed to not dissipate energy and ignored.

Reversible Process

Increasing the efficiency of stoon engines was an imfurtant techical problem in the 18'th and 19'th
centries. To answere this question Carnot developed the idea of a reversible thermodynamic process. A reversible thermodynamic process is an idealized limit of a process that occurs slow enough that at each step the thermodyramic system reaches a state described by an equation of state withart dissapating ener sy. A processes of this type is known as a quasistatk process.
A result of these assumptions is that the process can be reversed. That is it is possible for the system return to its original state. This upe of process does
conform to physical intuition since a cup of coffee that has cooled to room temeperature will not become warm again without the addition of energy since the process of cooling resulted in an unrecoverable dissipation of energy to the environment. Dissipative effects, such as fraction, are ignored' in the reverible process in an attempt to construct a process of the highest efficienry.
As an example of a reversible process consider once again the previous piston and chamber example.
If the force applied to the piston F → F → vec(F)\vec{F}F→ is increased by an infintesimal amount d F ¯ d F ¯ d bar(F)d \bar{F}dF¯. The gas in the cylinder will then be compressed by an infinitesimal amount d v = A d z d v = A d z dv=Adzd v=A d zdv=Adz, where A A AAA is the crossectional area of the cylinder. This will result in an increase in temperature and pressure because of the state change to utdU. If F → + d F → F → + d F → vec(F)+d vec(F)\vec{F}+d \vec{F}F→+dF→ is next clecreased by d F ¯ d F ¯ d bar(F)d \bar{F}dF¯ the gas in the cylinder will expland by d v = A d z d v = A d z dv=Adzd v=A d zdv=Adz and the temperature of the gas will decrease returning the piston to its original position and state since no energy was dissipated when the force was increased or decreased.
The position of the piston can be increased by an arbitrary amount with a finite state by the proceedure and then reversed to create a revesible process, Since it is assumed that the time required for each step is sufficient for the gas equation of state to reach utdll the process each step coud take an infinite amount of time resulting in a process that could take forever. The idea of a reversible process makes no statements cebout duration.
Carnot's Principle summarizes this result.
The most efficient thermodynamic process is reversible.
The result is analogys to ignoring dissipation in mechanics when constructing Newton's birst law describing ideal motion
unless a body is aded upon by a force it will remain at rest or in uniform motion in a stright line.
This statement is not obvious from everyday experience where forces acting on a object are never absent Naton's First law represents the limit of zero dissipotion in a manner simpled to Carnot's Theorem.
It should be noted that while a reverible process is quasistatic a quasistatic process need not be reversible since it can be dissipative.

Summay of Thermodynamic Processes

A thermodynamic process is a physical processes satisfying the following constraints
  1. Conservation of energy, First Law of Thermodynamics
Q = Δ u + ω Q = Δ u + ω Q=Delta u+omegaQ=\Delta u+\omegaQ=Δu+ω
Where Q = Q = Q=Q=Q= heat, A U = A U = AU=A U=AU= change in internal energy and ω = ω = omega=\omega=ω= work.
2) Satisfies an equation of state of the form
S ( u , P , T , v , … ) = 0 S ( u , P , T , v , … ) = 0 S(u,P,T,v,dots)=0S(u, P, T, v, \ldots)=0S(u,P,T,v,…)=0
where u = u = u=u=u= internal energy, p = p = p=p=p= pressure V = V = V=V=V= Volume, P = P = P=P=P= pressuree and any other relevent state variables.
3) Has an intitual and final state.
Further the following mutually exchesive constraints
  1. Reversible (assumes no dissipatio) quasistatic
  2. Not reversible (assumes dissipation) quasistatic
  3. Not reversible and not quasistatic
Application of First Law to simple Fluid
A simple fluid has an equation of slate trat can be expressed by assuming any two of the variables T T TTT, P P PPP or v v vvv are independent, where T T TTT is tempearature, P P PPP is pressure and V V VVV is volume.
The First Law of Thermodynamil in differential form is given by
d u = d Q − P d v ⇒ d Q = d u + P d v d u = d Q − P d v ⇒ d Q = d u + P d v {:[du=dQ-Pdv],[=>dQ=du+Pdv]:}\begin{aligned} & d u=d Q-P d v \\ \Rightarrow & d Q=d u+P d v \end{aligned}du=dQ−Pdv⇒dQ=du+Pdv
Now if the independent variables are assumed to be T T TTT and V V VVV then
u = u ( T , v ) u = u ( T , v ) u=u(T,v)u=u(T, v)u=u(T,v)
and
d u = ( ∂ U ∂ T ) V d T + ( ∂ U ∂ V ) T d V d u = ∂ U ∂ T V d T + ∂ U ∂ V T d V du=((del U)/(del T))_(V)dT+((del U)/(del V))_(T)dVd u=\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{V} d T+\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{T} d Vdu=(∂U∂T)VdT+(∂U∂V)TdV
where the subscripts which variable is held constant for differentiation.
Comparing with the first law gives
d Q − P d V = ( ∂ u ∂ T ) V d T + ( ∂ u ∂ V ) T d V ⇒ d Q = [ P + ( ∂ u ∂ V ) T ] d V + ( ∂ u ∂ T ) V d T d Q − P d V = ∂ u ∂ T V d T + ∂ u ∂ V T d V ⇒ d Q = P + ∂ u ∂ V T d V + ∂ u ∂ T V d T {:[dQ-PdV=((del u)/(del T))_(V)dT+((del u)/(del V))_(T)dV],[=>dQ=[P+((del u)/(del V))_(T)]dV+((del u)/(del T))_(V)dT]:}\begin{aligned} & d Q-P d V=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_{V} d T+\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial V}\right)_{T} d V \\ \Rightarrow & d Q=\left[P+\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial V}\right)_{T}\right] d V+\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_{V} d T \end{aligned}dQ−PdV=(∂u∂T)VdT+(∂u∂V)TdV⇒dQ=[P+(∂u∂V)T]dV+(∂u∂T)VdT
Next consider the case
where P P PPP are the independent variables then
U = U ( T , P ) U = U ( T , P ) U=U(T,P)U=U(T, P)U=U(T,P)
and
d u = ( ∂ u ∂ T ) P d T + ( ∂ u ∂ P ) T d P d u = ∂ u ∂ T P d T + ∂ u ∂ P T d P du=((del u)/(del T))_(P)dT+((del u)/(del P))_(T)dPd u=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_{P} d T+\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial P}\right)_{T} d Pdu=(∂u∂T)PdT+(∂u∂P)TdP
From the first law of Thermodynamics
d Q − P d V = ( ∂ u ∂ T ) P d T + ( ∂ u ∂ P ) T d P d Q − P d V = ∂ u ∂ T P d T + ∂ u ∂ P T d P dQ-PdV=((del u)/(del T))_(P)dT+((del u)/(del P))_(T)dPd Q-P d V=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_{P} d T+\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial P}\right)_{T} d PdQ−PdV=(∂u∂T)PdT+(∂u∂P)TdP
and since T T TTT and D D DDD are the independent variables
It must be that
V = V ( T , P ) V = V ( T , P ) V=V(T,P)V=V(T, P)V=V(T,P)
then
d V = ( ∂ V ∂ T ) P d T + ( ∂ V ∂ P ) T d P d V = ∂ V ∂ T P d T + ∂ V ∂ P T d P dV=((del V)/(del T))_(P)dT+((del V)/(del P))_(T)dPd V=\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} d T+\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_{T} d PdV=(∂V∂T)PdT+(∂V∂P)TdP
and substituting into the previous equation gives
d Q − P d V = d Q − P [ ( ∂ V ∂ T ) P d T + ( ∂ U ∂ P ) T d P ] = ( ∂ U ∂ T ) P d T + ( ∂ U ∂ P ) T d P ⇒ d Q = [ ( ∂ U ∂ T ) P + P ( ∂ V ∂ T ) P ] d T + [ ( ∂ U ∂ P ) T + P ( ∂ V ∂ P ) T ] d P d Q − P d V = d Q − P ∂ V ∂ T P d T + ∂ U ∂ P T d P = ∂ U ∂ T P d T + ∂ U ∂ P T d P ⇒ d Q = ∂ U ∂ T P + P ∂ V ∂ T P d T + ∂ U ∂ P T + P ∂ V ∂ P T d P {:[dQ-PdV=dQ-P[((del V)/(del T))_(P)dT+((del U)/(del P))_(T)dP]],[=((del U)/(del T))_(P)dT+((del U)/(del P))_(T)dP],[=>dQ=[((del U)/(del T))_(P)+P((del V)/(del T))_(P)]dT],[+[((del U)/(del P))_(T)+P((del V)/(del P))_(T)]dP]:}\begin{aligned} d Q-P d V=d Q-P\left[\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P} d T+\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_{T} d P\right] \\ =\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{P} d T+\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_{T} d P \\ \Rightarrow d Q=\left[\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{P}+P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P}\right] d T \\ +\left[\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_{T}+P\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_{T}\right] d P \end{aligned}dQ−PdV=dQ−P[(∂V∂T)PdT+(∂U∂P)TdP]=(∂U∂T)PdT+(∂U∂P)TdP⇒dQ=[(∂U∂T)P+P(∂V∂T)P]dT+[(∂U∂P)T+P(∂V∂P)T]dP
Finally consider the case where U Then P U  Then  P (U)/(" Then ")P\frac{U}{\text { Then }} PU Then P are the irdenendent.
u = u ( P , v ) u = u ( P , v ) u=u(P,v)u=u(P, v)u=u(P,v)
and
d u = ( ∂ u ∂ P ) v d P + ( ∂ u ∂ v ) P d v d u = ∂ u ∂ P v d P + ∂ u ∂ v P d v du=((del u)/(del P))_(v)dP+((del u)/(del v))_(P)dvd u=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial P}\right)_{v} d P+\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_{P} d vdu=(∂u∂P)vdP+(∂u∂v)Pdv
The first law gives
d Q − P d v = ( ∂ u ∂ P ) v d P + ( ∂ u ∂ v ) P d v d Q − P d v = ∂ u ∂ P v d P + ∂ u ∂ v P d v dQ-Pdv=((del u)/(del P))_(v)dP+((del u)/(del v))_(P)dvd Q-P d v=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial P}\right)_{v} d P+\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_{P} d vdQ−Pdv=(∂u∂P)vdP+(∂u∂v)Pdv
⇒ d Q = ( ∂ u ∂ P ) V d P + [ ( ∂ u ∂ V ) P + P ] d V ⇒ d Q = ∂ u ∂ P V d P + ∂ u ∂ V P + P d V =>dQ=((del u)/(del P))_(V)dP+[((del u)/(del V))_(P)+P]dV\Rightarrow d Q=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial P}\right)_{V} d P+\left[\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial V}\right)_{P}+P\right] d V⇒dQ=(∂u∂P)VdP+[(∂u∂V)P+P]dV
The thermal capacity of an object is defined by the derivative of Q Q QQQ with respect to T T TTT. The cases of V V VVV and is constant need to be considered seperately. First consider the dase where v v vvv is constant.

Now

V = constant ⇒ d V = 0 V =  constant  ⇒ d V = 0 V=" constant "=>dV=0V=\text { constant } \Rightarrow d V=0V= constant ⇒dV=0
so the first baw gives
d Q = d u d Q = d u dQ=dud Q=d udQ=du
Let C v C v C_(v)C_{v}Cv denote the thermal capacity at constant volume. It follows that
C V = ( d θ d T ) V = ( ∂ U ∂ T ) V C V = d θ d T V = ∂ U ∂ T V C_(V)=((d theta)/(dT))_(V)=((del U)/(del T))_(V)C_{V}=\left(\frac{d \theta}{d T}\right)_{V}=\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{V}CV=(dθdT)V=(∂U∂T)V
Similarhy let CP denote the thermal kapacity at constant pressure
C P = ( d Q d T ) P = ( ∂ u ∂ T ) P + P ( ∂ U ∂ T ) P C P = d Q d T P = ∂ u ∂ T P + P ∂ U ∂ T P C_(P)=((dQ)/(dT))_(P)=((del u)/(del T))_(P)+P((del U)/(del T))_(P)C_{P}=\left(\frac{d Q}{d T}\right)_{P}=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_{P}+P\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{P}CP=(dQdT)P=(∂u∂T)P+P(∂U∂T)P
The second term represents the heat required to expand the system. This effect is
Not present in C v C v C_(v)\mathrm{C}_{v}Cv since U U UUU is constant so there is no expansion.
The thermal capectity of one gram of a substance is dalled the specific neat.
Application to an Ideal lass
An ideal gas is obtained with the assumptions
  1. u = u ( T ) u = u ( T ) u=u(T)u=u(T)u=u(T)
  2. C v = C v = C_(v)=\mathrm{C}_{v}=Cv= constant
  3. P V − n R T = 0 P V − n R T = 0 PV-nRT=0P V-n R T=0PV−nRT=0
    where 3) is the equation of state where R R RRR is the ideal gas constant
R = 1.986 cal degree = 8.314 × 10 7 erg degree R = 1.986 cal  degree  = 8.314 × 10 7 erg  degree  {:[R=1.986quad(cal)/(" degree ")],[=8.314 xx10^(7)(erg)/(" degree ")]:}\begin{aligned} R & =1.986 \quad \frac{\mathrm{cal}}{\text { degree }} \\ & =8.314 \times 10^{7} \frac{\mathrm{erg}}{\text { degree }} \end{aligned}R=1.986cal degree =8.314×107erg degree 
and n n nnn is the number of moles of the gas. The differential form of the equation of asswming n n nnn is constant is
From the definition of C v C v CvC vCv it follows that
d u d T = C v d u d T = C v (du)/(dT)=C_(v)\frac{d u}{d T}=C_{v}dudT=Cv
⇒ U ( T ) = C V T + U 0 ⇒ U ( T ) = C V T + U 0 =>U(T)=C_(V)T+U_(0)\Rightarrow U(T)=C_{V} T+U_{0}⇒U(T)=CVT+U0
Where u 0 u 0 u_(0)u_{0}u0 is the energy at T = 0 T = 0 T=0T=0T=0.
Now the first law gives
d Q = d u + P d v = C v d T + P d v d Q = d u + P d v = C v d T + P d v {:[dQ=du+Pdv],[=CvdT+Pdv]:}\begin{aligned} d Q & =d u+P d v \\ & =C v d T+P d v \end{aligned}dQ=du+Pdv=CvdT+Pdv
From the equation of state it must be that
P d v + v d P = n R d T P d v + v d P = n R d T Pdv+vdP=nRdTP d v+v d P=n R d TPdv+vdP=nRdT

combining this result with the first law gives

d Q = C V d T + P d V = C V d T + n R d T − V d P = ( C V + n R ) d T − V d P d Q = C V d T + P d V = C V d T + n R d T − V d P = C V + n R d T − V d P {:[dQ=C_(V)dT+PdV],[=C_(V)dT+nRdT-VdP],[=(C_(V)+nR)dT-VdP]:}\begin{aligned} d Q & =C_{V} d T+P d V \\ & =C_{V} d T+n R d T-V d P \\ & =\left(C_{V}+n R\right) d T-V d P \end{aligned}dQ=CVdT+PdV=CVdT+nRdT−VdP=(CV+nR)dT−VdP
Now for a constant pressure
process
d P = 0 d P = 0 dP=0d P=0dP=0
It follows that
C P = ( d Q d T ) P = C V + n R C P = d Q d T P = C V + n R C_(P)=((dQ)/(dT))_(P)=C_(V)+nRC_{P}=\left(\frac{d Q}{d T}\right)_{P}=C_{V}+n RCP=(dQdT)P=CV+nR
let
K = C P C V = C V + n R C V = 1 + n R C V K = C P C V = C V + n R C V = 1 + n R C V K=(C_(P))/(C_(V))=(C_(V)+nR)/(C_(V))=1+(nR)/(C_(V))K=\frac{C_{P}}{C_{V}}=\frac{C_{V}+n R}{C_{V}}=1+\frac{n R}{C_{V}}K=CPCV=CV+nRCV=1+nRCV

Adiabatic Process for Ideal Ees

A thermodynamic process is adibadic is

d Q = 0 d Q = 0 dQ=0d Q=0dQ=0

Recall that for an ideal gas the first law has the form
d Q = C v d T + P d V d Q = C v d T + P d V dQ=C_(v)dT+PdVd Q=C_{v} d T+P d VdQ=CvdT+PdV
so for a adibadic process
C V d T + P d V = 0 C V d T + P d V = 0 C_(V)dT+PdV=0C_{V} d T+P d V=0CVdT+PdV=0
From the equation of state
P = n R T V P = n R T V P=(nRT)/(V)P=\frac{n R T}{V}P=nRTV
Substituting into the previous equation gives
C v d T + n R T v d v = 0 C v d T + n R T v d v = 0 C_(v)dT+(nRT)/(v)dv=0C_{v} d T+\frac{n R T}{v} d v=0CvdT+nRTvdv=0
⇒ 1 T d T + n R C V 1 V d V = 0 ⇒ 1 T d T + n R C V 1 V d V = 0 =>quad(1)/(T)dT+(nR)/(C_(V))(1)/(V)dV=0\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{T} d T+\frac{n R}{C_{V}} \frac{1}{V} d V=0⇒1TdT+nRCV1VdV=0
Integraling gives ln ⁡ T + n R C V ln ⁡ V = constant ⇒ ln ⁡ T + ln ⁡ V n R / C V = constant ⇒ T V n R C V = constant using gives = n R C V + 1 but T V K − 1 = constant and P V = n R T P V K = constant  Integraling gives  ln ⁡ T + n R C V ln ⁡ V =  constant  ⇒ ln ⁡ T + ln ⁡ V n R / C V =  constant  ⇒ T V n R C V =  constant   using   gives  = n R C V + 1  but  T V K − 1 =  constant   and  P V = n R T P V K =  constant  {:[" Integraling gives "],[qquad ln T+(nR)/(C_(V))ln V=" constant "],[=>ln T+ln V^(nR//C_(V))=" constant "],[=>TV^((nR)/(C_(V)))=" constant "],[" using "],[" gives "=(nR)/(C_(V))+1],[" but "TV^(K-1)=" constant "],[" and "PV=nRT],[PV^(K)=" constant "]:}\begin{aligned} & \text { Integraling gives } \\ & \qquad \ln T+\frac{n R}{C_{V}} \ln V=\text { constant } \\ & \Rightarrow \ln T+\ln V^{n R / C_{V}}=\text { constant } \\ & \Rightarrow T V^{\frac{n R}{C_{V}}}=\text { constant } \\ & \text { using } \\ & \text { gives }=\frac{n R}{C_{V}}+1 \\ & \text { but } T V^{K-1}=\text { constant } \\ & \text { and } P V=n R T \\ & P V^{K}=\text { constant } \end{aligned} Integraling gives ln⁡T+nRCVln⁡V= constant ⇒ln⁡T+ln⁡VnR/CV= constant ⇒TVnRCV= constant  using  gives =nRCV+1 but TVK−1= constant  and PV=nRTPVK= constant 
For a constant temperature grocess the relationgas law groes the relation
P V = constant P V =  constant  PV=" constant "P V=\text { constant }PV= constant 
Cyclic Thermodynamic Process
A thermodynamic process with with no change in interal energy is called cyclic. It follows that for a cyclic process, using
Δ u = 0 Δ u = 0 Delta u=0\Delta u=0Δu=0
the first law gives
Q = ω Q = ω Q=omegaQ=\omegaQ=ω
That is the work percformed by the process is equal to the heat absorbed by the process.
Physical Measurement of Heat and Internal Energy
The calorie is defined as the amount of heat required to raise the temperalure of I gram of water at atmospheric pressure by 1 degree. It follows that to naise n n nnn grams of water by 1 degree requires n n nnn calories of heat.
Let Δ u s Δ u s Deltau_(s)\Delta u_{s}Δus indicate the change in internal energy and ω c ω c omega_(c)\omega_{c}ωc the work performed by expansion when 1 gram of water increases in temperature by 1 degree at atmospheric pressure. The change in interal energy and work done when 1 grams of water is increased in temperature by 1 degree is
Δ u c = n Δ u c ω c = n ω c Δ u c = n Δ u c ω c = n ω c {:[Deltau_(c)=n Deltau_(c)],[omega_(c)=nomega_(c)]:}\begin{aligned} \Delta u_{c} & =n \Delta u_{c} \\ \omega_{c} & =n \omega_{c} \end{aligned}Δuc=nΔucωc=nωc
Air
1 Atmosphere Calorimeter
Consider the experiment shown in the diagram above. A system under study is placed inside a calorimeter. The walls of the calorimeter are assumed to be adibatic so that it is thermally isolated and it contains n n nnn grams of water at atmospheric pressure. A layer. of air awove the water is maintained at 1 atmosphere by a release value. Both water and air are 14 ∘ C 14 ∘ C 14^(@)C14^{\circ} \mathrm{C}14∘C. A system S S SSS is submerged in the water in a container with with walls that are thermally conducting.
Assume that a thermodynamic process in s s sss has a final state that leaves the water at 150 C . Let Δ u s Δ u s Deltau_(s)\Delta u_{s}Δus and Δ u c Δ u c Deltau_(c)\Delta u_{c}Δuc dende the change in interal energy resuting from the thermodynamic process for s s sss and the calorimeter water respectively.
The total change in interral energy is
Δ u = Δ u s + Δ u c Δ u = Δ u s + Δ u c Delta u=Deltau_(s)+Deltau_(c)\Delta u=\Delta u_{s}+\Delta u_{c}Δu=Δus+Δuc
The total work done is
ω = ω s + ω c ω = ω s + ω c omega=omega_(s)+omega_(c)\omega=\omega_{s}+\omega_{c}ω=ωs+ωc
Recall that the first law of thermodynamics is given by
Q = Δ u + ω Q = Δ u + ω Q=Delta u+omegaQ=\Delta u+\omegaQ=Δu+ω
for the system as a whole
Q total = 0 Q total  = 0 Q_("total ")=0Q_{\text {total }}=0Qtotal =0
since the calorimeter has adibadic walls.
It follows that
Δ u + ω = Δ u s + Δ u c + ω s + ω c = 0 ⇒ Δ u s + ω s = − ( Δ u c + ω c ) Δ u + ω = Δ u s + Δ u c + ω s + ω c = 0 ⇒ Δ u s + ω s = − Δ u c + ω c {:[Delta u+omega=Deltau_(s)+Deltau_(c)+omega_(s)+omega_(c)=0],[=>Deltau_(s)+omega_(s)=-(Deltau_(c)+omega_(c))]:}\begin{aligned} & \Delta u+\omega=\Delta u_{s}+\Delta u_{c}+\omega_{s}+\omega_{c}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \Delta u_{s}+\omega_{s}=-\left(\Delta u_{c}+\omega_{c}\right) \end{aligned}Δu+ω=Δus+Δuc+ωs+ωc=0⇒Δus+ωs=−(Δuc+ωc)
But the heat lost by s s sss is given by
Q s = Δ u s + ω s Q s = Δ u s + ω s Q_(s)=Deltau_(s)+omega_(s)Q_{s}=\Delta u_{s}+\omega_{s}Qs=Δus+ωs
so the heat gained by the calorimeter is
Q c = − Q s Q c = − Q s Q_(c)=-Q_(s)Q_{c}=-Q_{s}Qc=−Qs
Recall that for n n nnn grams of water
Δ u c = n Δ u c ω c = n ω c Δ u c = n Δ u c ω c = n ω c Deltau_(c)=n Deltau_(c)quadomega_(c)=nomega_(c)\Delta u_{c}=n \Delta u_{c} \quad \omega_{c}=n \omega_{c}Δuc=nΔucωc=nωc
so
Q s = − n ( Δ u c + ω c ) Q s = − n Δ u c + ω c Q_(s)=-n(Deltau_(c)+omega_(c))Q_{s}=-n\left(\Delta u_{c}+\omega_{c}\right)Qs=−n(Δuc+ωc)
it follows that the amount of heat lost by the process is proportional to n n nnn. Thus
n calories of heat have
been transfered by the system
to the water.

Carnot Cycle

The Carnot Cycle is a Cyclic thermodynamic operating between temperatures T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and T C T C T_(C)T_{C}TC where T H > T C T H > T C T_(H) > T_(C)T_{H}>T_{C}TH>TC that transforms neat into work
To construct a Carot Cycle consider a cyclic thermodynamic process constructed from two isothothemal processes with temperatures T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and T C T C T_(C)T_{C}TC and two adibatic processes. The first starts at temperature T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and ends at temperature T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc and the second starts at temperature T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc and ends at temperature T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH.
An example of this process is shown in the following figure where it is assumed that the thermodynamic system is a flueid which
bas a state function of the
f ( P , V , T ) = 0 f ( P , V , T ) = 0 f(P,V,T)=0f(P, V, T)=0f(P,V,T)=0

V

The intitial and final state

of the carnot cycle is indicated by state A in the figure. The first isothermal process is in contact with a heat reservior at temperature T t t T t t T_(tt)T_{t t}Ttt and transitutions between states A and B B BBB in the figure. In this transfion an amount of heat Q H Q H Q_(H)Q_{H}QH is added to the system and the gas expands from volume v A v A v_(A)v_{A}vA to v B v B v_(B)v_{B}vB and the pressure decreases from P A P A P_(A)P_{A}PA to P B P B P_(B)P_{B}PB perfoming work.
Next the gas, ho longer in contact with the heat
reservoir. expands adibatically from volume v B v B v_(B)v_{B}vB to v C v C v_(C)v_{C}vC,
Pressure decreases from P B P B P_(B)P_{B}PB
P c P c P_(c)P_{c}Pc and the temperature decreases from T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH to T C T C T_(C)T_{C}TC dong work. For the third step of the process
while in contact with a neat reservoir at themperadure T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc work is done on the gas compressing it isothermally from volume v c v c v_(c)v_{c}vc to v p v p v_(p)v_{p}vp, the pressure decreases from P C P C P_(C)P_{C}PC to P D P D P_(D)P_{D}PD and an amount of heat Q c Q c Q_(c)Q_{c}Qc is lost by the gas. Finally, while no longer in contact' with the heat reservoir at T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc work is once again performed on the gas compressing it adibadically from volume V D V D V_(D)V_{D}VD-to V A V A V_(A)V_{A}VA, the pressure increases from P D A P D A P_(D)^(A)P_{D}^{A}PDA 'to P A P A P_(A)P_{A}PA and the temeperadure increases from T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc to T + 1 T + 1 T_(+1)T_{+1}T+1, returning the gas to the initial state. The cycle can then be repeated.
Recall that the work done by a cyclic tuermodynamic process is given by

ω = Q ω = Q omega=Q\omega=Qω=Q

For the Camot Grale the total amount of neat absorbed by the process is given by
Q = Q H − Q C Q = Q H − Q C Q=Q_(H)-Q_(C)Q=Q_{H}-Q_{C}Q=QH−QC
where Q 2 Q 2 Q_(2)Q_{2}Q2 is negative since it idicates heat lost by the process. #t follows that
ω = Q H − Q C ω = Q H − Q C omega=Q_(H)-Q_(C)\omega=Q_{H}-Q_{C}ω=QH−QC
where ω ω omega\omegaω is equal to the area bounded by the carnot cycle. Since w> b it follows that
Q 4 > Q c Q 4 > Q c Q_(4) > Q_(c)Q_{4}>Q_{c}Q4>Qc

Efficiency of Thermody ramic Process

The efficiency of a cyclic thermodynamic process is defined by
η = ω Q out = 1 − Q out Q in η = ω Q out  = 1 − Q out  Q in  eta=(omega)/(Q_("out "))=1-(Q_("out "))/(Q_("in "))\eta=\frac{\omega}{Q_{\text {out }}}=1-\frac{Q_{\text {out }}}{Q_{\text {in }}}η=ωQout =1−Qout Qin 
Here Q out = Q H Q out  = Q H Q_("out ")=Q_(H)Q_{\text {out }}=Q_{H}Qout =QH and Q in = Q C Q in  = Q C Q_("in ")=Q_(C)Q_{\text {in }}=Q_{C}Qin =QC. It
follows that
η = ω Q c = 1 − Q c Q H η = ω Q c = 1 − Q c Q H eta=(omega)/(Q_(c))=1-(Q_(c))/(Q_(H))\eta=\frac{\omega}{Q_{c}}=1-\frac{Q_{c}}{Q_{H}}η=ωQc=1−QcQH
Since the Carnot Cycle is a thermodynamic process it is reversible. For the reversed process heat is absorbed at T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc and expelled at T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH. The work performed is given by
ω R = Q c − Q H < 0 ω R = Q c − Q H < 0 omega_(R)=Q_(c)-Q_(H) < 0\omega_{R}=Q_{c}-Q_{H}<0ωR=Qc−QH<0
It follows that the reversed process requires that work be done on the thermodynamic system.

Second Law of Thermodynamics

There are several consistent ways to state the second law of Thermodynamics. The most widehy known but least intuitive is that for any isolated thermodynamic system the entropy always increases. A derivation of this statement is the point of this work. To get there it is useful to consider more intuitive statements that are equivalent. The earliest version of the second Law of Thermodynamics was stated by Clausius,
A coder object never heats a hotter object.
This is an observation that is widely held and easily understard and serves as an excelent starting point to understand the equivalent statement cloout entropy always increasing.
In this statement cooling an object means transfering energy from the object and heating means transferring energy to the object It is
also understood that a cooler object has a lower temperature than a hotter object.
To dig deeper into this statement the idea of a Heat Reservoir is used. A Heat Reservoir is defined as,
A heat reservoir maintains the temperature regardless of the amount of heating or cooling.
When modeling thermodynamic processes a thermodynamic system
can be assumed to be in contact with a heat reservior to transfere energy. So the stated definition of a heat reservoir is equivalent to the assumption that heat reservoir has a much larger energy content than the thermodynamic system so that any energy loss or gain by the heat reseroor is neglisible compared to loss or gain of an equivalent amount of energy by the thermodynamic system.
In terms of heat reservoirs the second law of thermodynamics can by stated as
A thermodynamic process cannot transfere ehergy from a cooler heat reservoir to a warmer heat reservoir.

The first of the previous figures illustrates a thermodynamic process that transfers energy from a warmer to a cosler heat reservoir which is allowed by the secord law of thermodynamics and the second figure a thermodynamic process that transfers energy from a warmer to a cooler heat reservoir which is not allowed by the second lares of Thermodynamics.
Another statement of the second law of thermodynamics attributed to cord Kelum is
A thermodynamic process that extracts heat from a heat reservort and obes work is impossible.
Thermodynamic
The previous figure illustrates this process using heat reservoirs.
A thermodynamically impossible process is obtained by stating the reverse of a thermodynamic process that is not revesible. For example, flow of Heat from a heat reservoir with temperature T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH to a heat reservoir with temperature T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc where T H > T c T H > T c T_(H) > T_(c)T_{H}>T_{c}TH>Tc is irreversible the reverse of this proccess is not albwed leading to this statement of the second law
A process that cools a hotter reservoir while warming a cooler reservoir is not reversible
Equivalence of Claresius and Kelvin statements of the second law of Thermodynamics
The Clausius version of the second Law of Thermodymamics is
A coder object never heats a notter object.
The kelum version of the second Law of Thermod, namics is
A thermodynamic process that extracts heat from a heat reservor and obes work is impossible.
To prove the equivalence of the clausius and Kelum statements of the second law of Thermodynamics it must be shown that the Clausius statement implies the kelvin statement and that the keluin statement implies the Clausius statement. The proof is constructed using controposition. A contrpositive proof proves,
P → Q P → Q P rarr QP \rightarrow QP→Q
by proving the contrapositive statement
¬ Q → ¬ P ¬ Q → ¬ P not Q rarr not P\neg Q \rightarrow \neg P¬Q→¬P
Theorem: The Clausius and kelvin Statements of the second law of Thermodynamics are Equivalent.
First assume that the negation of the Keluin statement is true so that a process that extracls heat from a neat reservoir at any temperature can do work

T Reservoir

Dissipative Thermodynamic system
T F Reservoir ↓ Q F T F  Reservoir  ↓ Q F T_(F)" Reservoir "darr QFT_{F} \text { Reservoir } \downarrow Q FTF Reservoir ↓QF
Consider the dissipative quasistatic process shown in the figure. A quantity of heat Q Q QQQ is extracted from a heat reservoir at some temperature T T TTT by a dissipative thermodynamic process which converts the heat to work w w www which turns a wheel in contact with another reservoir at temperature T F T F T_(F)T_{F}TF. The work is dissipated by frictional forces as a suatity of heat Q F Q F Q_(F)Q_{F}QF. Since T T TTT is arbitrary it can be choosen such that T < T F T < T F T < T_(F)T<T_{F}T<TF. Thus the heat Q Q QQQ is transported to the warmer reservoir from the cooler reservoir which is the negation of the Clausius statement of the second law. It follows that the clausius statement of the second law implies the Keluin statement.
To complete the proof assume that the negation of the Clausius statement of the serond law is true
so that cooler objects heat warmer objects.
Consider the processes shown in the figure above. Since the negation of the Clausius statemement is true, assume that a quantily of heat Q in Q in  Q_("in ")Q_{\text {in }}Qin  is transported from the cooler heat reservoir to the warmer reservoir by conduction. A Carnot cycle operating between temperatures T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and T C T C T_(C)T_{C}TC then absorbs Q i n Q i n Q_(in)Q_{i n}Qin and exspells Qout and does work ω = Q out − Q in ω = Q out  − Q in  omega=Q_("out ")-Q_("in ")\omega=Q_{\text {out }}-Q_{\text {in }}ω=Qout −Qin . The net flux of heat from the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reservoir is zero while the T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc reservoir has a nel heat flux of Q in Q in  Q_("in ")Q_{\text {in }}Qin  - Q out. Q out.  Q_("out. ")Q_{\text {out. }}Qout.  It follows that the net result of the process is to transform Qcn-Qout expelled from the T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc reservoir to the work
produced by the Carnot cycle which is the negation of the keluin statement of the second law. It follows that the keluin statement of the second law implies the Clausius statement.
The desired resut is obtained. The kelvin and Clausius statements of the second law are equivalent.

Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature

A Carnot Cicle is an example of a cyclic thermodynamic process operating between two temperatures T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc where the work performed by the process is given by
ω = Q H − Q C ω = Q H − Q C omega=Q_(H)-Q_(C)\omega=Q_{H}-Q_{C}ω=QH−QC
Now consider an arbitrary cyclic thermodynamic process operating between T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc. The relation between the work performed remains valid.
Theorem 1: If ω > 0 ω > 0 omega > 0\omega>0ω>0 then Q H > 0 Q H > 0 Q_(H) > 0Q_{H}>0QH>0 and Q c > 0 Q c > 0 Q_(c) > 0Q_{c}>0Qc>0
The proof is by contradicition which makes an assumption that is shown to violate the Second Law of Thermodynamics.
First assume that Q c ⩽ 0 Q c ⩽ 0 Q_(c) <= 0Q_{c} \leqslant 0Qc⩽0 and consider the following thermodynamic process
A thermodynamic process opereates between two heat reservoirs at temperatures T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and T C T C T_(C)T_{C}TC where T H > T C T H > T C T_(H) > T_(C)T_{H}>T_{C}TH>TC. Since Q c ⩽ 0 Q c ⩽ 0 Q_(c) <= 0Q_{c} \leqslant 0Qc⩽0 the process absorbs heat from both reservoirs. If the reservors are are then cornected so that a quantity of heat Q c Q c Q_(c)Q_{c}Qc is conducted from the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reserveroir to the T e T e T_(e)T_{e}Te reservoir. The net flux of heat from the T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc is then zero, while the net flux of heat flux from the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reservoir is Q H + Q c Q H + Q c Q_(H)+Q_(c)Q_{H}+Q_{c}QH+Qc. The net result of the
process is to convert the heat Q H + Q C Q H + Q C Q_(H)+Q_(C)Q_{H}+Q_{C}QH+QC from the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reservoir to work. This violates the Kelum statement of the second law.
Trus Q c > 0 Q c > 0 Q_(c) > 0Q_{c}>0Qc>0 and since ω > 0 ω > 0 omega > 0\omega>0ω>0 it follows Q H > 0 Q H > 0 Q_(H) > 0Q_{H}>0QH>0, which is the desired result.
Now consider two thermodynamic processes operating between T 4 T 4 T_(4)T_{4}T4 and T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc. For the first engine the work done is given by
ω 1 = Q 1 H − Q 1 C ω 1 = Q 1 H − Q 1 C omega_(1)=Q_(1H)-Q_(1C)\omega_{1}=Q_{1 H}-Q_{1 C}ω1=Q1H−Q1C
for the second process
ω 2 = Q 2 H − Q 2 c ω 2 = Q 2 H − Q 2 c omega_(2)=Q_(2H)-Q_(2c)\omega_{2}=Q_{2 H}-Q_{2 c}ω2=Q2H−Q2c
The following theorems will be proved Theorem 2:
A. If the first process is revesible
Q 1 H Q 1 C ⩾ Q 2 H Q 2 C Q 1 H Q 1 C ⩾ Q 2 H Q 2 C (Q_(1H))/(Q_(1C)) >= (Q_(2H))/(Q_(2C))\frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{1 C}} \geqslant \frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{2 C}}Q1HQ1C⩾Q2HQ2C
B. If the second process is also rever sible
Q 1 H Q 1 C = Q 2 H Q 2 C Q 1 H Q 1 C = Q 2 H Q 2 C (Q_(1H))/(Q_(1C))=(Q_(2H))/(Q_(2C))\frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{1 C}}=\frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{2 C}}Q1HQ1C=Q2HQ2C
To construct a proof of the A portion of the theorem consider the figure above where two cyclic themodyamic
processes operating between temperatures T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH and T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc. The first process is assumed to be a quasistatic process running in reverse and for the A part of the proof no additional assumptions are made. Il is not nessisarly reversible.
Since process 1 is numing in reverse. So the work performed by the process is guen by
− ω 1 = Q 1 c − Q 2 H − ω 1 = Q 1 c − Q 2 H -omega_(1)=Q_(1c)-Q_(2H)-\omega_{1}=Q_{1 c}-Q_{2 H}−ω1=Q1c−Q2H
where ω 1 > 0 , Q 1 c > 0 , Q 1 + > 0 ω 1 > 0 , Q 1 c > 0 , Q 1 + > 0 omega_(1) > 0,Q_(1c) > 0,Q_(1+) > 0\omega_{1}>0, Q_{1 c}>0, Q_{1+}>0ω1>0,Q1c>0,Q1+>0 and Q I C < Q I H Q I C < Q I H Q_(IC) < Q_(IH)Q_{I C}<Q_{I H}QIC<QIH.
For process 2 the work is given by
ω 2 = Q 2 t t − Q 2 c ω 2 = Q 2 t t − Q 2 c omega_(2)=Q_(2tt)-Q_(2c)\omega_{2}=Q_{2 t t}-Q_{2 c}ω2=Q2tt−Q2c
where ω 2 > 0 , Q 2 H > 0 , Q 2 C > 0 ω 2 > 0 , Q 2 H > 0 , Q 2 C > 0 omega_(2) > 0,Q_(2H) > 0,Q_(2C) > 0\omega_{2}>0, Q_{2 H}>0, Q_{2 C}>0ω2>0,Q2H>0,Q2C>0 and Q 2 C < Q 2 H Q 2 C < Q 2 H Q_(2C) < Q_(2H)Q_{2 C}<Q_{2 H}Q2C<Q2H
Now, consider the ratio
Q 14 Q 21 t = N 2 N 1 ⇒ Q 2 H N 2 − Q 1 + N 1 = 0 Q 14 Q 21 t = N 2 N 1 ⇒ Q 2 H N 2 − Q 1 + N 1 = 0 (Q_(14))/(Q_(21 t))=(N_(2))/(N_(1))=>Q_(2H)N_(2)-Q_(1+)N_(1)=0\frac{Q_{14}}{Q_{21 t}}=\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \Rightarrow Q_{2 H} N_{2}-Q_{1+} N_{1}=0Q14Q21t=N2N1⇒Q2HN2−Q1+N1=0
Assume that it can be written as the ratio of two integers. For any value of

Q 1 H Q 2 H Q 1 H Q 2 H (Q_(1H))/(Q_(2H))\frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{2 H}}Q1HQ2H

values of N 1 N 1 N_(1)N_{1}N1 and N 2 N 2 N_(2)N_{2}N2 ca: be choosen that approximate the ratio to any desired accuracy. This is the clever trick that makes the proof possible.
Assume that process 1 is run through N N NNN, cycles and procces 2 through N 2 N 2 N_(2)\mathrm{N}_{2}N2 cycles. It then follows that the total work of both processes performed after completing N 1 N 1 N_(1)N_{1}N1 and N 2 N 2 N_(2)N_{2}N2 cycles is given by
ω Total = N 2 ω 2 − N 1 ω 1 ω Total  = N 2 ω 2 − N 1 ω 1 omega_("Total ")=N_(2)omega_(2)-N_(1)omega_(1)\omega_{\text {Total }}=N_{2} \omega_{2}-N_{1} \omega_{1}ωTotal =N2ω2−N1ω1
The total neat absorbed from the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reservoir is
Q H , Total = N 2 Q 2 H − N 1 Q 1 H Q H ,  Total  = N 2 Q 2 H − N 1 Q 1 H Q_(H," Total ")=N_(2)Q_(2H)-N_(1)Q_(1H)Q_{H, \text { Total }}=N_{2} Q_{2 H}-N_{1} Q_{1 H}QH, Total =N2Q2H−N1Q1H
and the total heat absorbed by the T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc reservoir is
Q c , Total = N 2 Q 2 c − N 1 Q 1 c Q c ,  Total  = N 2 Q 2 c − N 1 Q 1 c Q_(c," Total ")=N_(2)Q_(2c)-N_(1)Q_(1c)Q_{c, \text { Total }}=N_{2} Q_{2 c}-N_{1} Q_{1 c}Qc, Total =N2Q2c−N1Q1c
It follows that W total is given by W total is given by  W_("total is given by ")W_{\text {total is given by }}Wtotal is given by 
ω Total = Q H, Total − Q c, Total ω Total  = Q H, Total  − Q c, Total  omega_("Total ")=Q_("H, Total ")-Q_("c, Total ")\omega_{\text {Total }}=Q_{\text {H, Total }}-Q_{\text {c, Total }}ωTotal =QH, Total −Qc, Total 
Recall that from definition of N 1 N 1 N_(1)N_{1}N1 and N 2 N 2 N_(2)\mathrm{N}_{2}N2
Q H , Total = N 2 Q 2 H − N 1 Q 1 H = 0 Q H ,  Total  = N 2 Q 2 H − N 1 Q 1 H = 0 Q_(H," Total ")=N_(2)Q_(2H)-N_(1)Q_(1H)=0Q_{H, \text { Total }}=N_{2} Q_{2 H}-N_{1} Q_{1 H}=0QH, Total =N2Q2H−N1Q1H=0
Thus N 1 N 1 N_(1)N_{1}N1 and N 2 N 2 N_(2)N_{2}N2 are choosen such that the total heat change of the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reservoir is zero.
It follows that
W Total = − Q c , Total W Total  = − Q c ,  Total  W_("Total ")=-Q_(c," Total ")W_{\text {Total }}=-Q_{c, \text { Total }}WTotal =−Qc, Total 
It must be that ω Total ⩽ 0 ω Total  ⩽ 0 omega_("Total ") <= 0\omega_{\text {Total }} \leqslant 0ωTotal ⩽0 since if that were not the case the end result of N N NNN, reversed cycles of process 1 and N 2 N 2 N_(2)\mathrm{N}_{2}N2 cycles of process 2 would be to convert heat from the T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc reservoir to work which would violate the Kelvin statement of the second law of thermodynamics.
It follows that
ω Total ⩽ 0 ⇒ Q c , Total ⩾ 0 ω Total  ⩽ 0 ⇒ Q c ,  Total  ⩾ 0 omega_("Total ") <= 0=>Q_(c," Total ") >= 0\omega_{\text {Total }} \leqslant 0 \Rightarrow Q_{c, \text { Total }} \geqslant 0ωTotal ⩽0⇒Qc, Total ⩾0
but
Q c , Total = N 2 Q 2 c − N 1 Q 1 c Q c ,  Total  = N 2 Q 2 c − N 1 Q 1 c Q_(c," Total ")=N_(2)Q_(2c)-N_(1)Q_(1c)Q_{c, \text { Total }}=N_{2} Q_{2 c}-N_{1} Q_{1 c}Qc, Total =N2Q2c−N1Q1c
thus it follows that
N 2 Q 2 c − N 1 Q 1 c ⩾ 0 ⇒ N 2 Q 2 c ⩾ N 1 Q 1 c ⇒ Q 2 c Q 1 c ⩾ N 1 N 2 N 2 Q 2 c − N 1 Q 1 c ⩾ 0 ⇒ N 2 Q 2 c ⩾ N 1 Q 1 c ⇒ Q 2 c Q 1 c ⩾ N 1 N 2 {:[N_(2)Q_(2c)-N_(1)Q_(1c) >= 0],[=>quadN_(2)Q_(2c) >= N_(1)Q_(1c)],[=>quad(Q_(2c))/(Q_(1c)) >= (N_(1))/(N_(2))]:}\begin{aligned} & N_{2} Q_{2 c}-N_{1} Q_{1 c} \geqslant 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad N_{2} Q_{2 c} \geqslant N_{1} Q_{1 c} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_{2 c}}{Q_{1 c}} \geqslant \frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}} \end{aligned}N2Q2c−N1Q1c⩾0⇒N2Q2c⩾N1Q1c⇒Q2cQ1c⩾N1N2
recall that
Q 1 H Q 21 t = N 2 N 1 ⇒ Q 2 H Q 1 H = N 1 N 2 Q 1 H Q 21 t = N 2 N 1 ⇒ Q 2 H Q 1 H = N 1 N 2 (Q_(1H))/(Q_(21 t))=(N_(2))/(N_(1))=>(Q_(2H))/(Q_(1H))=(N_(1))/(N_(2))\frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{21 t}}=\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \Rightarrow \frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{1 H}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}Q1HQ21t=N2N1⇒Q2HQ1H=N1N2
and the desired result is obtained
Q 2 c Q 1 c ⩾ Q 2 H Q 1 H ⇒ Q 1 H Q 1 c ⩾ Q 2 H Q 2 c Q 2 c Q 1 c ⩾ Q 2 H Q 1 H ⇒ Q 1 H Q 1 c ⩾ Q 2 H Q 2 c {:[quad(Q_(2c))/(Q_(1c)) >= (Q_(2H))/(Q_(1H))],[=>quad(Q_(1H))/(Q_(1c)) >= (Q_(2H))/(Q_(2c))]:}\begin{array}{r} \quad \frac{Q_{2 c}}{Q_{1 c}} \geqslant \frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{1 H}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{1 c}} \geqslant \frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{2 c}} \end{array}Q2cQ1c⩾Q2HQ1H⇒Q1HQ1c⩾Q2HQ2c
To obtain the B B BBB part of the theorem first assume that process 2 is reversible and process 1 is not. A similar result for this case is obtained by switching processes 1 and 2 in the previous result to obtain
Q 2 H Q 2 c ⩾ Q 1 H Q 1 c Q 2 H Q 2 c ⩾ Q 1 H Q 1 c (Q_(2H))/(Q_(2c)) >= (Q_(1H))/(Q_(1c))\frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{2 c}} \geqslant \frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{1 c}}Q2HQ2c⩾Q1HQ1c
If both processes are revesbible this inequality and the previous result must be true. This is possible only if equality holds and part B B BBB of the theorem is proven.
Q 1 H Q 2 C = Q 2 H Q 2 C Q 1 H Q 2 C = Q 2 H Q 2 C (Q_(1H))/(Q_(2C))=(Q_(2H))/(Q_(2C))\frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{2 C}}=\frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{2 C}}Q1HQ2C=Q2HQ2C
This resut is valid for any two revesible processes. It follows that for any revesible processes operating between the temperatures T H = T c T H = T c T_(H)=T_(c)T_{H}=T_{c}TH=Tc this equality holds and that for any non reversible process operating between the same temperatures the ratio is bounded by the reversible process

value.

Now since all revessible processes have the same value for the ratio
Q H Q C Q H Q C (Q_(H))/(Q_(C))\frac{Q_{H}}{Q_{C}}QHQC
it must be independent of any propertres of the process other than the temperatures T C T C T_(C)T_{C}TC and T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH. It follows that
Q H Q c = g ( T H , T c ) Q H Q c = g T H , T c (Q_(H))/(Q_(c))=g(T_(H),T_(c))\frac{Q_{H}}{Q_{c}}=g\left(T_{H}, T_{c}\right)QHQc=g(TH,Tc)
Theorem 3: The function
f ( t 1 , t 2 ) = Q 2 Q 1 f t 1 , t 2 = Q 2 Q 1 f(t_(1),t_(2))=(Q_(2))/(Q_(1))f\left(t_{1}, t_{2}\right)=\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}f(t1,t2)=Q2Q1
has the following property
f ( t 1 , t 2 ) = f ( t 0 , t 2 ) f ( t 0 , t 1 ) f t 1 , t 2 = f t 0 , t 2 f t 0 , t 1 f(t_(1),t_(2))=(f(t_(0),t_(2)))/(f(t_(0),t_(1)))f\left(t_{1}, t_{2}\right)=\frac{f\left(t_{0}, t_{2}\right)}{f\left(t_{0}, t_{1}\right)}f(t1,t2)=f(t0,t2)f(t0,t1)
where t 0 , t 1 t 0 , t 1 t_(0),t_(1)t_{0}, t_{1}t0,t1 and t 2 t 2 t_(2)t_{2}t2 are arbitrary values of temperature.
Consider the two reversible thermodynamic processes shown in the figure. Process operates between temeperatures t 1 t 1 t_(1)t_{1}t1 and t 0 t 0 t_(0)t_{0}t0 and process 2 opereales between t 2 t 2 t_(2)t_{2}t2 and t 0 t 0 t_(0)t_{0}t0. Also, t 1 > t 0 t 1 > t 0 t_(1) > t_(0)t_{1}>t_{0}t1>t0 and t 2 > t 0 t 2 > t 0 t_(2) > t_(0)t_{2}>t_{0}t2>t0. Now process 1 absorbs a quantity of heat Q 1 Q 1 Q_(1)Q_{1}Q1 from reservoir t 1 t 1 t_(1)t_{1}t1 and ejects a quantity of heat Q 0 Q 0 Q_(0)Q_{0}Q0 into reservoir to. Similary process 2 absorbs a quantity of heat Q 2 Q 2 Q_(2)Q_{2}Q2 from reservoir t 2 t 2 t_(2)t_{2}t2 and ejects a quanity of heat Q 0 Q 0 Q_(0)Q_{0}Q0 into reservoir to equal to the quantity of heat ejected by process 1.
Now,
Q 1 Q 0 = f ( t 0 , t 1 ) Q 1 Q 0 = f t 0 , t 1 (Q_(1))/(Q_(0))=f(t_(0),t_(1))\frac{Q_{1}}{Q_{0}}=f\left(t_{0}, t_{1}\right)Q1Q0=f(t0,t1)
and
Q 2 Q 0 = f ( t 0 , t 2 ) Q 2 Q 0 = f t 0 , t 2 (Q_(2))/(Q_(0))=f(t_(0),t_(2))\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{0}}=f\left(t_{0}, t_{2}\right)Q2Q0=f(t0,t2)
It follows that
Q 2 Q 1 = f ( t 0 , t 2 ) f ( t 0 , t 2 ) Q 2 Q 1 = f t 0 , t 2 f t 0 , t 2 (Q_(2))/(Q_(1))=(f(t_(0),t_(2)))/(f(t_(0),t_(2)))\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=\frac{f\left(t_{0}, t_{2}\right)}{f\left(t_{0}, t_{2}\right)}Q2Q1=f(t0,t2)f(t0,t2)
Next consider the case where process 1 runs in reverse so that the total
flux of heat from the T 0 T 0 T_(0)T_{0}T0 reservoir is zero. These coupled processes are equivalent to a single process operating between t 2 t 2 t_(2)t_{2}t2 and t 1 t 1 t_(1)t_{1}t1. To see this the work done by process 1 is given by
ω 1 = Q 0 − Q 1 ω 1 = Q 0 − Q 1 omega_(1)=Q_(0)-Q_(1)\omega_{1}=Q_{0}-Q_{1}ω1=Q0−Q1
and the work obne by process 2 is given by
ω 2 = Q 2 − Q γ ω 2 = Q 2 − Q γ omega_(2)=Q_(2)-Q_(gamma)\omega_{2}=Q_{2}-Q_{\gamma}ω2=Q2−Qγ
eliminating Q 0 Q 0 Q_(0)Q_{0}Q0 gives
ω 2 = Q 2 − ω 1 − Q 1 ⇒ ω 2 + ω 1 = Q 2 − Q 1 ω 2 = Q 2 − ω 1 − Q 1 ⇒ ω 2 + ω 1 = Q 2 − Q 1 {:[omega_(2)=Q_(2)-omega_(1)-Q_(1)],[=>omega_(2)+omega_(1)=Q_(2)-Q_(1)]:}\begin{aligned} & \omega_{2}=Q_{2}-\omega_{1}-Q_{1} \\ \Rightarrow & \omega_{2}+\omega_{1}=Q_{2}-Q_{1} \end{aligned}ω2=Q2−ω1−Q1⇒ω2+ω1=Q2−Q1
Let the work for the combined process be
ω = ω 2 + ω 1 ω = ω 2 + ω 1 omega=omega_(2)+omega_(1)\omega=\omega_{2}+\omega_{1}ω=ω2+ω1
then
ω = Q 2 − Q 1 ω = Q 2 − Q 1 omega=Q_(2)-Q_(1)\omega=Q_{2}-Q_{1}ω=Q2−Q1
It follows that
Q 2 Q 1 = f ( t 1 , t 2 ) Q 2 Q 1 = f t 1 , t 2 (Q_(2))/(Q_(1))=f(t_(1),t_(2))\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=f\left(t_{1}, t_{2}\right)Q2Q1=f(t1,t2)
comparing this with the previous result gives the desired result
f ( t 1 , t 2 ) = f ( t 0 , t 2 ) f ( t 0 , t 1 ) f t 1 , t 2 = f t 0 , t 2 f t 0 , t 1 {:[f(t_(1),t_(2))=f(t_(0),t_(2))],[f(t_(0),t_(1))]:}\begin{array}{r} f\left(t_{1}, t_{2}\right)=f\left(t_{0}, t_{2}\right) \\ f\left(t_{0}, t_{1}\right) \end{array}f(t1,t2)=f(t0,t2)f(t0,t1)
The function f ( t 0 , t ) f t 0 , t f(t_(0),t)f\left(t_{0}, t\right)f(t0,t) can be considered a transformation to an absoute temperature scale thus. since f ( t 1 , t 2 ) f t 1 , t 2 f(t_(1),t_(2))f\left(t_{1}, t_{2}\right)f(t1,t2) is defined by a ratio the transformation can be multiplied by an arbitrary constant k k kkk
T = k f ( t 0 , t ) T = k f t 0 , t T=kf(t_(0),t)T=k f\left(t_{0}, t\right)T=kf(t0,t)
For example, the kelun temperature scale is an absolute since zero temperature is really zero temperature. This is not the case for the Fatrenheit and Celcius scales. The transformations from Fahrenheight and Celcius to the kelum scale are defined by
T ( t f ) = 5 9 ( t f + 459.67 ) T ( t c ) = t c + 273.15 T t f = 5 9 t f + 459.67 T t c = t c + 273.15 {:[T(t_(f))=(5)/(9)(t_(f)+459.67)],[T(t_(c))=t_(c)+273.15]:}\begin{aligned} & T\left(t_{f}\right)=\frac{5}{9}\left(t_{f}+459.67\right) \\ & T\left(t_{c}\right)=t_{c}+273.15 \end{aligned}T(tf)=59(tf+459.67)T(tc)=tc+273.15
Now let
T 1 = k f ( t 0 , t 1 ) T 2 = k f ( t 0 , t 2 ) T 1 = k f t 0 , t 1 T 2 = k f t 0 , t 2 {:[T_(1)=kf(t_(0),t_(1))],[T_(2)=kf(t_(0),t_(2))]:}\begin{aligned} & T_{1}=k f\left(t_{0}, t_{1}\right) \\ & T_{2}=k f\left(t_{0}, t_{2}\right) \end{aligned}T1=kf(t0,t1)T2=kf(t0,t2)
and it follows that for any reversible process
Q 2 Q 1 = T 2 T 1 ⇒ Q 2 T 2 = Q 1 T 1 Q 2 Q 1 = T 2 T 1 ⇒ Q 2 T 2 = Q 1 T 1 {:[(Q_(2))/(Q_(1))=(T_(2))/(T_(1))],[=>(Q_(2))/(T_(2))=(Q_(1))/(T_(1))]:}\begin{aligned} \frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} & =\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{Q_{2}}{T_{2}} & =\frac{Q_{1}}{T_{1}} \end{aligned}Q2Q1=T2T1⇒Q2T2=Q1T1

Thermodynamic Process Efficiency

Recall that the efficiency of a thermodynamic process is defined by
η = 1 − Q C Q H η = 1 − Q C Q H eta=1-(Q_(C))/(Q_(H))\eta=1-\frac{Q_{C}}{Q_{H}}η=1−QCQH
It was previously shown that for for a revesible process, denoted by process 1, and nonreversible process, denoted by process 2 the following inequalify is satisfied.
Q 1 H Q 1 c ⩾ Q 2 H Q 2 c ⇒ Q 2 c Q 2 H ⩾ Q 1 c Q 1 H      Q 1 H Q 1 c ⩾ Q 2 H Q 2 c ⇒      Q 2 c Q 2 H ⩾ Q 1 c Q 1 H {:[,(Q_(1H))/(Q_(1c)) >= (Q_(2H))/(Q_(2c))],[=>,(Q_(2c))/(Q_(2H)) >= (Q_(1c))/(Q_(1H))]:}\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{Q_{1 H}}{Q_{1 c}} \geqslant \frac{Q_{2 H}}{Q_{2 c}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{Q_{2 c}}{Q_{2 H}} \geqslant \frac{Q_{1 c}}{Q_{1 H}} \end{array}Q1HQ1c⩾Q2HQ2c⇒Q2cQ2H⩾Q1cQ1H
it follows that
1 − Q 1 c Q 14 ⩾ 1 − Q 2 c Q 1 c 1 − Q 1 c Q 14 ⩾ 1 − Q 2 c Q 1 c 1-(Q_(1c))/(Q_(14)) >= 1-(Q_(2c))/(Q_(1c))1-\frac{Q_{1 c}}{Q_{14}} \geqslant 1-\frac{Q_{2 c}}{Q_{1 c}}1−Q1cQ14⩾1−Q2cQ1c
Thus a reversible process opperating between temperatures T H > T C T H > T C T_(H) > T_(C)T_{H}>T_{C}TH>TC will place an uper bound on the efficiency of any non reversible
process opperating between the
same two temperatures.
For any two reversible processes T t > T c T t > T c T_(t) > T_(c)T_{t}>T_{c}Tt>Tc ing between temperatures
Q 2 c Q 2 H = Q 1 c Q 1 H Q 2 c Q 2 H = Q 1 c Q 1 H (Q_(2c))/(Q_(2H))=(Q_(1c))/(Q_(1H))\frac{Q_{2 c}}{Q_{2 H}}=\frac{Q_{1 c}}{Q_{1 H}}Q2cQ2H=Q1cQ1H
it follows that they will have the same efficiency.

Thermodynamic Entropy

Previously it was shown that for a reversible process operating between two temperatures T > T T > T T > TT>TT>T
Q H T H = Q c T c ⇒ Q H T H ⋅ Q c T c = 0 Q H T H = Q c T c ⇒ Q H T H ⋅ Q c T c = 0 {:[(Q_(H))/(T_(H))=(Q_(c))/(T_(c))],[=>(Q_(H))/(T_(H))*(Q_(c))/(T_(c))=0]:}\begin{aligned} & \frac{Q_{H}}{T_{H}}=\frac{Q_{c}}{T_{c}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{Q_{H}}{T_{H}} \cdot \frac{Q_{c}}{T_{c}}=0 \end{aligned}QHTH=QcTc⇒QHTH⋅QcTc=0
Consider a system s s sss composed of n n nnn heat reservoirs at temperatures T 1 , T 2 , … , T n T 1 , T 2 , … , T n T_(1),T_(2),dots,T_(n)T_{1}, T_{2}, \ldots, T_{n}T1,T2,…,Tn. Let the amounts of heat exchanged with the reservoirs be denoted by Q i Q i Q_(i)Q_{i}Qi were Q i Q i Q_(i)Q_{i}Qi is positive if is the system absorbs the heat and negative if the system exspells the heat.
It will be shown that
∑ i = 1 n Q i T i ⩽ 0 ∑ i = 1 n   Q i T i ⩽ 0 sum_(i=1)^(n)(Q_(i))/(T_(i)) <= 0\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Q_{i}}{T_{i}} \leqslant 0∑i=1nQiTi⩽0
To prove this assertion another heat reservoir at an arbitrary temperature T 0 T 0 T_(0)T_{0}T0 is added and n n nnn carnot cycles operating belween the temperatures T 1 , T 2 , … , T n T 1 , T 2 , … , T n T_(1),T_(2),dots,T_(n)T_{1}, T_{2}, \ldots, T_{n}T1,T2,…,Tn and T 0 T 0 T_(0)T_{0}T0 are also added.
Thermodynamic Process
Consider the figure where the system S S SSS is composed of n / 2 n / 2 n//2n / 2n/2 cyclic thermodynamic processes colored green. The ith process in S S SSS absorbs and expells heat Q i Q i Q_(i)Q_{i}Qi and Q i + 1 Q i + 1 Q_(i+1)Q_{i+1}Qi+1 at temperatures T i T i T_(i)T_{i}Ti and Titi respectively. n n nnn Carot cycles are added. The ith carnot cycle operates between temperature reservoirs T 0 T 0 T_(0)T_{0}T0 and T i T i T_(i)T_{i}Ti and absorbs or expells heat Q i , 0 Q i , 0 Q_(i,0)Q_{i, 0}Qi,0 from the T 0 T 0 T_(0)T_{0}T0 reservoir that is sufficient to absorb or expell Q i Q i Q_(i)Q_{i}Qi heat from reservoir T i T i T_(i)T_{i}Ti so that the total heat flux from reservoir T i T i T_(i)T_{i}Ti is zero. Now each Carnot excle sctisfies the relation
Q i 0 T 0 = Q i T i Q i 0 T 0 = Q i T i (Q_(i0))/(T_(0))=(Q_(i))/(T_(i))\frac{Q_{i 0}}{T_{0}}=\frac{Q_{i}}{T_{i}}Qi0T0=QiTi
It follows that
Q i , 0 = T 0 T c Q i Q i , 0 = T 0 T c Q i Q_(i,0)=(T_(0))/(T_(c))Q_(i)Q_{i, 0}=\frac{T_{0}}{T_{c}} Q_{i}Qi,0=T0TcQi
Now if the system, s s sss, is run for one cycle and each of the Camot Oples is nun one time. The total heat absorbed by the carnot
Cycles from the To reservoir is given by
Q 0 = ∑ i = 1 n Q i , 0 = T 0 ∑ i = 1 n Q i T i Q 0 = ∑ i = 1 n   Q i , 0 = T 0 ∑ i = 1 n   Q i T i Q_(0)=sum_(i=1)^(n)Q_(i,0)=T_(0)sum_(i=1)^(n)(Q_(i))/(T_(i))Q_{0}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{i, 0}=T_{0} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Q_{i}}{T_{i}}Q0=∑i=1nQi,0=T0∑i=1nQiTi
Since the neat flux from each of the T i T i T_(i)T_{i}Ti reservoirs for one cycle is replaced by the i'th Carnot acce the total heat flux from each reservoir is zero it follows that total heat absorbed by both S S SSS and the carot cyles in one cycle is Qo. It follows that the work done is given by
ω = Q 0 ω = Q 0 omega=Q_(0)\omega=Q_{0}ω=Q0
Now if ω > 0 ω > 0 omega > 0\omega>0ω>0 all of the work done is from heat expeld at at a single temperature To. This violates the kelvin statement of the second Law of Thermodynamics. It follows that
ω ⩽ 0 ⇒ Q 0 ⩽ 0 ω ⩽ 0 ⇒ Q 0 ⩽ 0 omega <= 0=>Q_(0) <= 0\omega \leqslant 0 \Rightarrow Q_{0} \leqslant 0ω⩽0⇒Q0⩽0
so
∑ i = 1 n Q i T i ≤ 0 ∑ i = 1 n   Q i T i ≤ 0 sum_(i=1)^(n)(Q_(i))/(T_(i)) <= 0\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Q_{i}}{T_{i}} \leq 0∑i=1nQiTi≤0
If the system S S SSS is reversible the reverse cycle will have
Q 0 ⩾ 0 Q 0 ⩾ 0 Q_(0) >= 0Q_{0} \geqslant 0Q0⩾0
Since the vidates the Kelurn Statement of the second law it must be that
Q 0 = 0 Q 0 = 0 Q_(0)=0Q_{0}=0Q0=0
50
∑ i = 1 n Q i T i = 0 ∑ i = 1 n   Q i T i = 0 sum_(i=1)^(n)(Q_(i))/(T_(i))=0\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Q_{i}}{T_{i}}=0∑i=1nQiTi=0
In the limit n → ∞ T n → ∞ T n rarr ooquad Tn \rightarrow \infty \quad Tn→∞T and Q Q QQQ become continuous. The result obtained when S S SSS is not reversible becomes
∮ δ Q T ≤ 0 ∮ δ Q T ≤ 0 oint(delta Q)/(T) <= 0\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0∮δQT≤0
and if S S SSS is reversible
∮ δ Q T = 0 ∮ δ Q T = 0 oint(delta Q)/(T)=0\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}=0∮δQT=0
Where the integral is evaluated over one complete cycle.
Here a distinction is made between exact and inexact differentrals. A exact differential is a function, such as the internal energy which can have the form of a differentiable function such as u ( T , V ) u ( T , V ) u(T,V)u(T, V)u(T,V) it follows that differentials will have the form
d u = ( ∂ u ∂ T ) V d T + ( ∂ U ∂ V ) T d V d u = ∂ u ∂ T V d T + ∂ U ∂ V T d V du=((del u)/(del T))_(V)dT+((del U)/(del V))_(T)dVd u=\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_{V} d T+\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{T} d Vdu=(∂u∂T)VdT+(∂U∂V)TdV
It follows that the change in u u uuu in a transition from state A A AAA to B B BBB is given by
Δ u = ∫ A B d u = u ( B ) − u ( A ) Δ u = ∫ A B   d u = u ( B ) − u ( A ) Delta u=int_(A)^(B)du=u(B)-u(A)\Delta u=\int_{A}^{B} d u=u(B)-u(A)Δu=∫ABdu=u(B)−u(A)
the integral idependent of the path taken throug the τ , V τ , V tau,V\tau, Vτ,V space.
A inexact differential cannot be written as the derivative of a function. An example is the heat and work differentials in the first law
δ Q = d u − δ ω δ Q = d u − δ ω delta Q=du-delta omega\delta Q=d u-\delta \omegaδQ=du−δω
The differentials δ ω δ ω delta omega\delta \omegaδω and δ Q δ Q delta Q\delta QδQ depend on the path taken between the states A A AAA and B B BBB. For example isothermal and adibatic expansions for a ideal gas have different relations between δ ω δ ω delta omega\delta \omegaδω and δ Q δ Q delta Q\delta QδQ though the internal energy change is the same. It follows that in general δ ω δ ω delta omega\delta \omegaδω and δ Q δ Q delta Q\delta QδQ cannot be written as the derivative of a function.

Entropy

Recall that all cyclic reversible thermodynamic processes satisfy
∮ δ Q T = 0 ∮ δ Q T = 0 oint(delta Q)/(T)=0\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}=0∮δQT=0
where the integral is evaluated over one complete cycle.
Now consider an intitial state A A AAA and a final state B B BBB. Many reversible processes that start at A A AAA and end at B B BBB are possible. If the processes can be represented by a pressure and volume plot any continuous function of the form P ( v ) P ( v ) P(v)P(v)P(v) that connects the points is a possible reversible process.
Possible reversible processes that begin at A A AAA and end at B B BBB are shown in the previous plot.
Consider B B _(B){ }_{B}B the integral
∫ A B δ Q T ∫ A B   δ Q T int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T)\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}∫ABδQT
It will be shown that it has the following property
Theorem: The value of the integral is the same for all reversible procosses.
To prove this theorem consider two reversible thermodynamic processes between the states A and B
Now it was previously shown that
∮ δ Q T = 0 ∮ δ Q T = 0 oint(delta Q)/(T)=0\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}=0∮δQT=0
But
∮ δ Q T = ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) I + ( ∫ B A δ Q T ) I = O ⇒ ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) I = − ( ∫ B A δ Q T ) I ⇒ ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) I = ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) I ∮ δ Q T = ∫ A B   δ Q T I + ∫ B A   δ Q T I = O ⇒ ∫ A B   δ Q T I = − ∫ B A   δ Q T I ⇒ ∫ A B   δ Q T I = ∫ A B   δ Q T I {:[oint(delta Q)/(T)=(int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(I)+(int_(B)^(A)(delta Q)/(T))_(I)=O],[=>(int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(I)=-(int_(B)^(A)(delta Q)/(T))_(I)],[=>(int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(I)=(int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(I)]:}\begin{aligned} & \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}=\left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{I}+\left(\int_{B}^{A} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{I}=O \\ \Rightarrow & \left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{I}=-\left(\int_{B}^{A} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{I} \\ \Rightarrow & \left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{I}=\left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{I} \end{aligned}∮δQT=(∫ABδQT)I+(∫BAδQT)I=O⇒(∫ABδQT)I=−(∫BAδQT)I⇒(∫ABδQT)I=(∫ABδQT)I
Where the first intergral is evaluated over process I and the second over process II. The last step follows from the assumed reversibility of process II. It follows that,
∫ A B δ Q T ∫ A B   δ Q T int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T)\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}∫ABδQT
is independent of the process connecting
the state A and B. the state A A AAA and B B BBB.
This property motivates definition of entropy
Δ S = S ( B ) − S ( A ) = ∫ A B δ Q T Δ S = S ( B ) − S ( A ) = ∫ A B   δ Q T Delta S=S(B)-S(A)=int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T)\Delta S=S(B)-S(A)=\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}ΔS=S(B)−S(A)=∫ABδQT
where the quantity Δ S = S ( B ) − S ( A ) Δ S = S ( B ) − S ( A ) Delta S=S(B)-S(A)\Delta S=S(B)-S(A)ΔS=S(B)−S(A) is independent of the reversible Process that connects the states A A AAA and B B BBB and the integral.
Now
S ( A ) − S ( A ) = ∫ A B d S = ∫ A B δ Q T ⇒ ∫ A B ( d S − δ Q T ) = 0 ⇒ d S = δ Q T ⇒ δ Q = T d S S ( A ) − S ( A ) = ∫ A B   d S = ∫ A B   δ Q T ⇒ ∫ A B   d S − δ Q T = 0 ⇒ d S = δ Q T ⇒ δ Q = T d S {:[S(A)-S(A)=int_(A)^(B)dS=int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T)],[=>int_(A)^(B)(dS-(delta Q)/(T))=0],[=>dS=(delta Q)/(T)],[=>delta Q=TdS]:}\begin{aligned} & S(A)-S(A)=\int_{A}^{B} d S=\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T} \\ & \Rightarrow \int_{A}^{B}\left(d S-\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow d S=\frac{\delta Q}{T} \\ & \Rightarrow \delta Q=T d S \end{aligned}S(A)−S(A)=∫ABdS=∫ABδQT⇒∫AB(dS−δQT)=0⇒dS=δQT⇒δQ=TdS

Theorem: If the integral

∫ A B δ Q T ∫ A B   δ Q T int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T)\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}∫ABδQT
is evaluated over a nonreverible process connecting the states A and B
S ( B ) − S ( A ) ⩾ ∫ A B δ Q T S ( B ) − S ( A ) ⩾ ∫ A B   δ Q T S(B)-S(A) >= int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T)S(B)-S(A) \geqslant \int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}S(B)−S(A)⩾∫ABδQT
Consider the two processes shown in the figure
P
Here the process NR is nonreversible but still can be represented by a continuous curve P ( v ) P ( v ) P(v)P(v)P(v) and R R RRR is a reversible process. A cyclic process is constructed by first transitioning from state A A AAA to B B BBB using the nonreversible process and then returning from state B to A using the resersible processes. It was previously shown that for a nonreversible aydic process
§ δ Q T ≤ 0 § δ Q T ≤ 0 §(delta Q)/(T) <= 0\S \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0§δQT≤0
It follows that
∮ δ Q T = ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) N R + ( ∫ B A δ Q T ) R ⩽ 0 ⇒ ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) N R ⩽ − ( ∫ B A δ Q T ) R = ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) N R ⩽ ( ∫ A B δ Q T ) R = S ( B ) − S ( A ) = Δ S ∮ δ Q T = ∫ A B   δ Q T N R + ∫ B A   δ Q T R ⩽ 0 ⇒ ∫ A B   δ Q T N R ⩽ − ∫ B A   δ Q T R = ∫ A B   δ Q T N R ⩽ ∫ A B   δ Q T R = S ( B ) − S ( A ) = Δ S {:[oint(delta Q)/(T)=(int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(NR)+(int_(B)^(A)(delta Q)/(T))_(R) <= 0],[=>(int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(NR) <= -(int_(B)^(A)(delta Q)/(T))_(R)],[=(int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(NR) <= (int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(R)],[=S(B)-S(A)=Delta S]:}\begin{aligned} & \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}=\left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{N R}+\left(\int_{B}^{A} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{R} \leqslant 0 \\ \Rightarrow & \left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{N R} \leqslant-\left(\int_{B}^{A} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{R} \\ = & \left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{N R} \leqslant\left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{R} \\ = & S(B)-S(A)=\Delta S \end{aligned}∮δQT=(∫ABδQT)NR+(∫BAδQT)R⩽0⇒(∫ABδQT)NR⩽−(∫BAδQT)R=(∫ABδQT)NR⩽(∫ABδQT)R=S(B)−S(A)=ΔS
Thus the desired result is obtained
( ∫ A B δ Q T ) N R ⩽ S ( B ) − S ( A ) = Δ S ∫ A B   δ Q T N R ⩽ S ( B ) − S ( A ) = Δ S (int_(A)^(B)(delta Q)/(T))_(NR) <= S(B)-S(A)=Delta S\left(\int_{A}^{B} \frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{N R} \leqslant S(B)-S(A)=\Delta S(∫ABδQT)NR⩽S(B)−S(A)=ΔS
This expression is true in general where equality holds for resersible processes.
∫ A B δ Q T ⩽ Δ S ∫ A B   δ Q T ⩽ Δ S int_(A)^(B)delta(Q)/(T) <= Delta S\int_{A}^{B} \delta \frac{Q}{T} \leqslant \Delta S∫ABδQT⩽ΔS
for the special case of an isolated system d Q = 0 d Q = 0 dQ=0d Q=0dQ=0 so
Δ S ⩾ 0 Δ S ⩾ 0 Delta S >= 0\Delta S \geqslant 0ΔS⩾0
Thus for any thermodynamic process operating on an isolated system the entropy can never decrease.
This statement is true only for isolated systems. In general it is possible to constuat a thermodynamic process the results in a decrease in entropy.
As an example consider the flow of a quantity of heat Q Q QQQ from a heat reservoir at temperature T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH to a reservoir at temperature T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc where T H > T C T H > T C T_(H) > T_(C)T_{H}>T_{C}TH>TC

T H T H  T_("H ")T_{\text {H }}TH  Reservoir

T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc Reservoir

A thermodynamic process absorbs a quantity of heat Q Q QQQ from the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reservoir and expells the same quantity of heat into a reservoir at temperature Tc. Recall that a heat reservoir
can give up any amount of heat and maintain its temperature. It fullows that the entropy change of the T H T H T_(H)T_{H}TH reservoir is
Δ S T H = ∫ δ Q T H = 1 T H ∫ δ Q = − Q T H Δ S T H = ∫ δ Q T H = 1 T H ∫ δ Q = − Q T H DeltaS_(T_(H))=int(delta Q)/(T_(H))=(1)/(T_(H))int delta Q=-(Q)/(T_(H))\Delta S_{T_{H}}=\int \frac{\delta Q}{T_{H}}=\frac{1}{T_{H}} \int \delta Q=-\frac{Q}{T_{H}}ΔSTH=∫δQTH=1TH∫δQ=−QTH
and the change in entropy of the T c T c T_(c)T_{c}Tc reservoir is
Δ S T C = Q T C Δ S T C = Q T C DeltaS_(T_(C))=(Q)/(T_(C))\Delta S_{T_{C}}=\frac{Q}{T_{C}}ΔSTC=QTC
The total change in entropy of the system is
Δ S Total = Δ S T c + Δ S T H = Q ( 1 T l − 1 T H ) Δ S Total  = Δ S T c + Δ S T H = Q 1 T l − 1 T H DeltaS_("Total ")=DeltaS_(T_(c))+DeltaS_(T_(H))=Q((1)/(T_(l))-(1)/(T_(H)))\Delta S_{\text {Total }}=\Delta S_{T_{c}}+\Delta S_{T_{H}}=Q\left(\frac{1}{T_{l}}-\frac{1}{T_{H}}\right)ΔSTotal =ΔSTc+ΔSTH=Q(1Tl−1TH)
since T H > T C T H > T C T_(H) > T_(C)T_{H}>T_{C}TH>TC it follows that
Δ S Total > 0 Δ S Total  > 0 DeltaS_("Total ") > 0\Delta S_{\text {Total }}>0ΔSTotal >0
This demonstrates the equivalence of the clausius statement of the second Law of Thermodynamics and the entropic statement.
The entropy of an isolated system can never decrease

Maximum Entropy

Since Δ S ⩾ 0 Δ S ⩾ 0 Delta S >= 0\Delta S \geqslant 0ΔS⩾0 for any process in a isolated system if the entropy if the entropy is maximum the state of the system cannot change its thermodynamic state since any change would result in a decrease in entropy; This property is important in statistical mechanks since it defines an optimization problem used to determine the equilibrium probability distribution of the constituents of a system.
First law of Thermodynamics
Prevously it was shown that
δ Q ≤ τ d S δ Q ≤ τ d S delta Q <= tau dS\delta Q \leq \tau d SδQ≤τdS
The first Law of Thermodynamics in differential form was shown to be given by
δ Q = d u − P d V ⩽ T d S ⇒ d u ⩽ T d S − P d V δ Q = d u − P d V ⩽ T d S ⇒ d u ⩽ T d S − P d V {:[delta Q=du-PdV <= TdS],[=>quaddu <= TdS-PdV]:}\begin{aligned} & \delta Q=d u-P d V \leqslant T d S \\ \Rightarrow \quad & d u \leqslant T d S-P d V \end{aligned}δQ=du−PdV⩽TdS⇒du⩽TdS−PdV
where equality holds for a reversible
process.

The Free Energy

In mechanics work is related to differences in potential energy,
ω = − Δ u ω = − Δ u omega=-Delta u\omega=-\Delta uω=−Δu
where u u uuu is a potential energy function
Recall the first law
Δ u = ω + Q ⇒ ω = − Δ u + Q Δ u = ω + Q ⇒ ω = − Δ u + Q {:[Delta u=omega+Q],[=>omega=-Delta u+Q]:}\begin{aligned} \Delta u & =\omega+Q \\ \Rightarrow \omega & =-\Delta u+Q \end{aligned}Δu=ω+Q⇒ω=−Δu+Q
Which is close to the mechanicle version but with the advition of the heat term
Now thermodynamic processes often occur when in thermal contact with the enviroment which can modled as a heat reservoir at temperature T E T E T_(E)T_{E}TE as well as other heat sources. Now the quantity of heat Q E Q E Q_(E)Q_{E}QE extracted from the enuironment is bounded by

since T E T E T_(E)T_{E}TE is a heat reservoir extraction of heat Q E Q E Q_(E)Q_{E}QE will not change the temperature. It follows that
Q E = ∫ δ Q E ⩽ T E ∫ d s = T E Δ S ⇒ Q E ⩽ T E Δ S Q E = ∫ δ Q E ⩽ T E ∫ d s = T E Δ S ⇒ Q E ⩽ T E Δ S {:[Q_(E)=int deltaQ_(E) <= T_(E)int ds=T_(E)Delta S],[=>quadQ_(E) <= T_(E)Delta S]:}\begin{aligned} & Q_{E}=\int \delta Q_{E} \leqslant T_{E} \int d s=T_{E} \Delta S \\ \Rightarrow \quad & Q_{E} \leqslant T_{E} \Delta S \end{aligned}QE=∫δQE⩽TE∫ds=TEΔS⇒QE⩽TEΔS
Thus the heat extracted from the enviornment is bounded. The first law becomes
ω ⩽ − Δ u + T E Δ S = − ( Δ u − T E Δ S ) ω ⩽ − Δ u + T E Δ S = − Δ u − T E Δ S omega <= -Delta u+T_(E)Delta S=-(Delta u-T_(E)Delta S)\omega \leqslant-\Delta u+T_{E} \Delta S=-\left(\Delta u-T_{E} \Delta S\right)ω⩽−Δu+TEΔS=−(Δu−TEΔS)
This inequality places an upper bound on the work that can be performed by the processes
The free energy is defined by
F = U − T S F = U − T S F=U-TSF=U-T SF=U−TS
since T E T E T_(E)T_{E}TE is assumed constant
Δ F = Δ U − T E Δ S Δ F = Δ U − T E Δ S Delta F=Delta U-T_(E)Delta S\Delta F=\Delta U-T_{E} \Delta SΔF=ΔU−TEΔS
thus the free energy gives the desired relationship between work and a potential
ω ⩽ − Δ F ω ⩽ − Δ F omega <= -Delta F\omega \leqslant-\Delta Fω⩽−ΔF
equality holds for roversible process.
